name: Honda CRX (EF)
reliability: 9/10 for sheer durability — the D-series is about as robust and simple as engines get, and at ~2,100 lbs there's very little to stress. These will run essentially forever on basic maintenance. The real enemy is rust and age, not mechanical failure: clean, un-rotted examples are genuinely hard to find now. One myth to correct — the US-market Si is NOT VTEC (the B16-powered SiR was JDM/Euro only), which is exactly why it became the definitive lightweight VTEC-swap platform.
cost_to_own: Insurance: Low · Maintenance ~$300/yr (cheap and simple) · Values are climbing for rust-free, unmodified examples — the CRX has become a bona fide collectible
popular_mods: A VTEC engine swap (B16/B18 or a D-series VTEC) — the classic CRX build, since the US Si came non-VTEC
Coilovers or lowering springs + sway bars — the featherweight chassis loves suspension work
Header + exhaust + intake for the D16
Lightweight wheels with sticky tires — huge impact on such a light car
Honest take: a rust-free, stock Si is now worth preserving — swap a rougher shell and leave the clean ones alone

generations.1st Gen (1984–1987): The original wedge-shaped economy sport hatch that started the CRX legend — simpler, lighter, and pure 80s.
generations.2nd Gen EF (1988–1991): The definitive CRX — HF (economy, ~50 mpg), DX, and the Si (105 hp in 1988, 108 hp from 1990 with a revised cam). Two-seat, sub-2,200 lb, and endlessly tunable.
generations.JDM/Euro SiR: The version US buyers wish they got — the 1.6L B16A VTEC (~150 hp), which is why so many US cars end up with a VTEC swap.
generations.Successor: Replaced by the Civic del Sol (targa) for 1993 — a heavier, softer take on the idea.

common_issues: Rust — rockers, floors, hatch, and rear quarters; on a 35-year-old car this is the single biggest thing to inspect
Worn suspension bushings and mounts that dull the light, tossable feel
Cracked dashboards and faded, brittle interior plastics from sun exposure
Many surviving cars are modified — finding an unmolested Si is the real challenge
maintenance_tips: Inspect for rust first and thoroughly — a rust-free shell is worth more than any mechanical spec
Keep up the timing belt interval and periodic valve-clearance checks on the D16
Refresh tired bushings and mounts to restore the classic CRX feel
Parts are cheap and plentiful — this is one of the most affordable classics to keep running
If it's a clean, stock Si, preserve its originality; build a rougher car instead
